UPDATES
Updates in Version 2.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers:
APP-1
• Footnote a modified: FDA added a Black Box warning to the capecitabine label, recommending to test patients for genetic variants of DPYD prior to 
initiating capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary. For information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity, see the 
Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer.
MS-1
• The Discussion section, which reflects the recommendations in the algorithm, has been added.

a For information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity, see the
Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer.
b Use of FDG-PET/CT is only considered in rare circumstances where CT findings 
are indeterminate or when CT with contrast is not possible. See Principles of 
Imaging (APP-A). 
c Colonoscopy is needed for a confirmed appendiceal neoplasm to rule out other 
primary lesions. This could be done concurrently with the other diagnostic 
studies, but should not delay treatment.
d Thorough review of operative notes is encouraged upon confirmation of 
appendiceal neoplasm in order to rule out more aggressive disease (eg, 
perforation, ascites present, signs of mucin).
e Diagnostic laparoscopy should be performed by a surgeon with experience in this 
disease process.
f See Principles of Surgery and CRS/IPCT (APP-C) for circumstances where a 
right hemicolectomy may be recommended.
g For peritoneal-only disease, image-guided biopsy is preferred for lesions that 
are safely accessible percutaneously when a non-surgical approach is preferred 
to confirm the diagnosis (eg, due to comorbidities or extraperitoneal disease). If 
inaccessible, surgical biopsy may be needed for diagnostic confirmation.

Localized disease (appendix only)
h Primary and metastatic disease are evaluated separately. See Principles of 
Pathology and Molecular Review (APP-B).
i Lesions with extra-appendiceal acellular mucin are considered low risk for 
dissemination. Lesions with extra-appendiceal mucin-containing neoplastic 
epithelium are considered high risk for recurrence or peritoneal dissemination 
and the peritoneal-only disease pathway would be followed.
j Lesions containing ≤50% signet-ring cells are classified as mucinous 
adenocarcinomas with signet-ring cells. Tumors containing >50% signet-ring 
cells are classified as signet-ring cell carcinomas. Signet-ring cell carcinomas are 
associated with a high level of metastasis and worse prognosis.
k Lesions containing ≤50% of mucin are classified as non-mucinous 
adenocarcinomas. Tumors containing >50% mucin are considered mucinous 
adenocarcinomas. 
l UCs only show epithelial marker expression if there is no histologic, 
immunohistochemical, or molecular indicators of the tissue of origin. Nagtegaal 
ID, et al. Histopathology 2020;76:182-188.
m Biomarker testing is recommended, although there may be a higher rate of 
assay failure if specimen has low cellularity. See Principles of Pathology and 
Molecular Review (APP-B).

header: PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING1-5
content:
General
• It is important to note that imaging may be negative in the setting of peritoneal metastases, or that it may underestimate the peritoneal disease volume. 
Direct visualization (laparoscopic evaluation when feasible) should be performed for confirmation in the staging setting and when recurrence is 
suspected clinically and imaging findings are negative.
• The mainstay of staging imaging should consist of a CT of the C/A/P OR non-contrast CT of the chest with MRI of the A/P. 
Evaluate local extent of tumor, presence of associated inflammation and/or perforation, and infiltration of adjacent structures and regional metastases.
Evaluate for distant metastases (ie, lymph nodes, lung, liver, peritoneum, other organs)
CT exam should be performed with oral and IV iodinated contrast, unless contraindicated.
If IV iodinated contrast is contraindicated due to severe allergy, then CT of the chest without contrast and MRI of the A/P with IV gadolinium-based 
contrast agent (GBCA) can be obtained instead.
In patients with chronic renal failure (estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] <30 mL/min) who are not on dialysis, IV contrast material is also 
contraindicated, and IV GBCA such as gadofosveset trisodium, gadoxetate disodium, gadobenate dimeglumine, or gadoteridol may be used.
If both IV iodinated contrast and IV GBCA are contraindicated, CT of the chest without contrast and MRI of the A/P without contrast should be 
considered. PET/CT may also be considered as an alternate.
Consider MRI of the liver in cases where better resolution of indeterminate liver lesions is needed and/or MRI of select cases of hepatic metastases 
when planning metastasectomy or liver-directed therapies. In these cases, utilization of hepatocyte-specific GBCA is preferred for precise localization 
and quantification of metastases.
FDG-PET/CT
• FDG-PET/CT is usually not indicated. In select cases (ie, neuroendocrine neoplasm), DOTATATE PET/CT should be considered.
FDG-PET does not supplant a contrast-enhanced diagnostic CT or MRI and can be used to evaluate an equivocal finding on a contrast-enhanced CT 
or MRI, but should not be the preferred imaging study.
• FDG-PET may under-appreciate disease burden, especially for mucinous tumors.
Monitoring
• For treatment response monitoring, typically a CT of the A/P (in cases of resected localized disease), or CT of the C/A/P (in cases of resected 
metastatic disease) with oral and IV contrast, or non-contrast CT of the chest with MRI of the A/P should be used.
For patients who have had liver-directed therapy (ablation-based, ie, radiofrequency ablation [RFA], microwave ablation [MWA]; radiation-based, ie, 
radioembolization, stereotactic body radiation therapy [SBRT]), MRI of the liver is preferred over CT for the assessment of treatment response.
In select cases FDG-PET may be indicated to assess residual tumor viability if curative liver resection is contemplated, but should be done in 
partnership with a contrasted CT or MRI.
For mucinous tumors, MRI of the A/P may be preferred over CT as mucinous implants may be easier to distinguish from adjacent structures due to 
their T2 signal.

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
APP-B
1 OF 4
Appendiceal Cancer Appropriate for Resection
• Primary appendiceal neoplasms are rare and most often found incidentally during an appendectomy following clinical presentation of acute appendicitis.
• Individual management and treatment of primary appendiceal cancers are dependent on classification, grading, and staging. 
• According to the most recent WHO classification of tumors, appendiceal epithelial neoplasms are categorized as follows1:
Serrated lesions and polyps
Mucinous neoplasm
Adenocarcinoma
GCA
Neuroendocrine neoplasms
Background: Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasms
• There is much ongoing debate over terminology and classification of mucinous neoplasms due to the fact that even low-grade neoplasms can result in 
pseudomyxomatous peritonei and lack of consensus criteria for invasion into the appendix. 
• Current terminology includes low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN), high-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (HAMN), low-grade 
mucinous carcinoma peritonei, high-grade mucinous carcinoma peritonei, and high-grade mucinous carcinoma peritonei with signet ring cells. 
• Older terminology that is no longer recommended includes: mucinous tumor of uncertain malignant potential, mucinous cystadenoma of the appendix, 
borderline tumor of the appendix, mucinous cystadenocarcinoma of the appendix, disseminated peritoneal adenomucinosis, and peritoneal mucinous 
carcinomatosis with or without signet ring cells.1,2,3
• However, some pathologists prefer to use the term "mucinous cystadenoma" to refer to entirely submitted mucinous tumors with no mural or extra-
appendiceal mucin. If mural changes are present, such as disruption or atrophy of the muscularis mucosa, these lesions are best classified as 
LAMN.4,5
Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN):
 ◊LAMNs are characterized by tall mucinous epithelial cells in villous or flat arrangement, which invade with a distinct pushing border in contrast to 
infiltrative glands associated with desmoplasia. 
 ◊LAMNs are most often associated with atrophy of the appendiceal wall, loss of lymphoid tissue, and effacement of the muscularis mucosa, which 
can be helpful in differentiating other entities on the histologic differential diagnosis such as ruptured diverticula.4,5 
 ◊Mucin pools are common and can extend into the serosa and beyond. 
 ◊According to the 9th version of the AJCC, LAMNs confined to the muscularis propria, including acellular mucin or mucinous epithelium, are staged 
as carcinoma in situ (pTis). pT1 and pT2 are not applicable in LAMN. 
 ◊Acellular mucin or mucinous epithelium that extends into the subserosa or serosa is classified as pT3 or pT4a, respectively.6 
 ◊LAMNs are almost always microsatellite stable (MSS), BRAF V600E negative, and frequently harbor KRAS mutations.2

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
High-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (HAMN):
 ◊HAMNs retain some similar findings as LAMN but show progression to a more aggressive lesion with the acquisition of high-grade cytologic 
features and complex architecture. 
 ◊As such, HAMNs are staged as mucinous adenocarcinomas, although HAMNs are rare and robust data are limited.2
Mucinous carcinoma peritonei:
 ◊Extra-appendiceal mucin is characterized as acellular or with neoplastic cells that can be low grade, high grade, or high grade with signet ring cells. 
 ◊The presence or absence of neoplastic cells and the degree of cytologic atypia have prognostic implications in that acellular mucin confined to the 
right lower quadrant has a low risk of progression whereas mucin with high-grade neoplastic epithelium with signet ring cells is associated with a 
much worse prognosis.2,7
Appendiceal adenocarcinoma (AA):
 ◊Outside of LAMN and pseudomyxoma peritonei, other primary AAs are rare and can arise from several distinct precursor lesions such as mucinous 
neoplasms, intestinal-type adenomas, and GCAs. 
 ◊These adenocarcinomas often demonstrate abundant extracellular mucin but are distinguished from mucinous neoplasms by a few key differences. 
 ◊	These tumors are highly cellular, have high-grade nuclear features, and show an infiltrative growth pattern with desmoplastic response.8,9 
 ◊Primary mucinous adenocarcinomas are rarely microsatellite unstable (microsatellite instability-high [MSI-H]). However, non-mucinous subtypes 
have been shown to be MSI-H.10
 ◊Mucinous subtypes are associated with better outcomes than non-mucinous subtypes. However, higher histologic grade in mucinous subtypes is 
associated with lower overall 5-year survival rates.11
Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA):
 ◊These tumors have been historically classified as NETs but are now recognized as a distinct clinicopathologic entity with biologic potential more 
similar to adenocarcinomas than NETs.9
 ◊Older terminology includes goblet cell carcinoid, goblet cell type adenocarcinoid, mucinous carcinoid, and crypt cell carcinoma. 
 ◊Microscopically, GCAs are characterized by deep mucosal and submucosal circumferential infiltration without associated surface epithelial 
dysplasia. 
 ◊Extension into the muscularis propria and serosa is commonly observed. 
 ◊GCAs are defined by the presence of a low-grade component comprised of small nests, clusters, and cords of goblet cells. Paneth cells and 
neuroendocrine cells may be present but are not required for a diagnosis of GCA. 
 ◊The presence of infiltrating single or small clusters of mucinous or non-mucinous cells, complex cribriform glands, or solid sheets of cells or signet 
ring cells are evidence of a high-grade component.

APP-B
3 OF 4
Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) (continued):
 ◊Recognizing low- and high-grade patterns is critical, as grading GCA depends on the percentages of each component.
– Grade 1 and 2 tumors demonstrate >75% and 50%–75% of tubular or clustered growth, respectively.
– Grade 3 tumors reveal <50% low-grade pattern and >50% of any combination of the high-grade pattern.12,13
 ◊As GCAs are often deeply invasive, patients with low-grade histology may present with evidence of metastasis. 
 ◊Interestingly, GCAs have different molecular profiles from other AAs and neuroendocrine neoplasms in that lower rates of KRAS and APC mutations 
are consistently reported. However, microsatellite instability (MSI) and high tumor mutational burden (TMB-H) are also rare in these tumors.14,15
Appendiceal neuroendocrine neoplasms:
 ◊Neuroendocrine neoplasms arising in the appendix include well-differentiated NETs, poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas (NECs), and 
MiNENs.16
 ◊Please see Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors.
Biomarker Testing
• Biomarker testing, including:
RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 status; mismatch repair (MMR) or MSI status (if not previously done)
Testing should be conducted as part of multigene panel testing (MGPT), which would identify rare and actionable mutations and fusions such as 
POLE/POLD1, RET, and NTRK 1/2/3.
For more information on biomarker testing, see the Principles of Pathologic and Molecular Review in the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND CRS/IPCT
content:
• Localized Disease:
Appendiceal tumors are heterogenous groups of tumors. Knowledge about the different pathologic types is key to appropriate surgical management.
Appendiceal tumors are rare but they are more common among patients with complicated appendicitis.
Histologies at lower risk of hematogenous spread, including LAMN and HAMN:
 ◊Negative margin appendectomy is largely considered to be sufficient. Ileocecectomy may be considered when appendectomy or extended 
appendectomy is not feasible.
 ◊Management of HAMN is controversial with some advocating for right colectomy.
 ◊Presence of perforation is a risk factor for peritoneal recurrence and warrants closer surveillance.
Histologies at higher risk of hematogenous spread, including adenocarcinoma: 
 ◊Right hemicolectomy with adequate lymphadenectomy is recommended.
There is no clear role for routine second look laparoscopy in the absence of other suggestive risk factors for recurrent disease.
• Peritoneal Disease:
In cases of incidental peritoneal mucinous or non-mucinous disease during appendectomy or surgical exploration, the focus should be on obtaining 
a diagnosis rather than extensive resection. Diagnosis can be confirmed by appendectomy and/or biopsy of the most solid- appearing peritoneal 
implants.
Preoperative imaging may underestimate the extent of disease in the peritoneal cavity. 
Staging laparoscopic exploration may be helpful in estimating the extent of peritoneal disease (PCI) and extent of surgery that will be required.
Patients deemed possible surgical candidates should be evaluated and treated by an experienced peritoneal surface malignancy surgeon for 
candidacy for CRS/IPCT. Expertise in peritoneal resections, multivisceral resections, and organ preservation techniques is key.
 ◊Candidacy for CRS/IPCT is determined through a multidisciplinary evaluation of the patient's overall health, disease biology and extent, and tumor 
resectability.
The extent of CRS should be individualized with the focus on optimal cytoreduction with organ preservation.
For surgical candidates, optimal cytoreduction (not debulking) with completeness of cytoreduction (CC) score of 0 or 1 is recommended.
Mitomycin C is the most commonly used agent for IPCT. Oxaliplatin is another option, but is less preferred, especially in patients who have received or 
will be receiving IV oxaliplatin.1,2
Repeat CRS/IPCT may be considered for patients with a history of previous CRS, particularly those with low-grade peritoneal tumors and/or limited 
peritoneal spread. For high-grade disease, the time since prior surgery should be taken into consideration. 
In cases where disease is incompletely cytoreduced or resected or CRS is aborted, consider clinical trial, systemic therapy, or best supportive care.
APP-C

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND CRS/IPCT
content:
• No Extraperitoneal Metastasis:
LAMN/HAMN Primary: 
 ◊The majority of mucinous appendiceal (non-adenocarcinoma) neoplasms are less likely to respond to systemic therapy.
 ◊CRS/IPCT is recommended for low-grade (and select high-grade) mucinous tumors.
 ◊Optimal cytoreduction should be pursued when possible. 
 ◊Palliative CRS/IPCT may be considered for unresectable disease in patients who are otherwise surgical candidates.
AA/GCA/UC-NOS:
 ◊Surgical candidates are suggested to receive perioperative systemic therapy for up to 6 months. 
 ◊Regular multidisciplinary review is recommended to assess surgical candidacy and optimal timing of surgery, recognizing that patients may have 
poor response to systemic therapy but still have disease that is amenable to CRS.
 ◊Additional systemic therapy may be considered for patients who are not resectable at initial diagnosis with the possibility of converting to resectable 
disease.
 ◊The PCI score and CC score should be reported for cytoreductive surgery.
 ◊Surgery is discouraged for high PCI.
 ◊If a patient is not a candidate for surgery, treatment should follow metastatic colon cancer guidelines.
• Extraperitoneal Disease:
Liver surface metastasis (scalloping) is not considered extraperitoneal disease. 
Management should follow systemic therapy guidelines with palliative measures used as needed for refractory symptoms.
Palliative debulking surgery can be considered for disease not responding or poorly responding to systemic therapy, but may be of limited benefit.
APP-C
2 OF 2

a For systemic therapy references, see Regimens and Dosing (APP-D 4 of 11).
b For infection risk, monitoring, and prophylaxis recommendations for targeted therapies, see INF-A in the Oncology Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Cancer- 
Related Infections.
c An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy in the Oncology Guidelines.
d Irinotecan should be used with caution in patients with Gilbert syndrome or elevated serum bilirubin. There is a commercially available test for UGT1A1. Guidelines for 
use in clinical practice have not been established.
e Discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3 to 4 months of therapy (or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity) while maintaining other agents 
until time of progression. Oxaliplatin may be reintroduced if it was discontinued for neurotoxicity rather than for disease progression.
f There is no clear evidence for anti-EGFR therapy, even among RAS/RAF WT cancers.
g FOLFIRINOX is recommended instead of FOLFOXIRI because FOLFOXIRI uses a high dose of fluorouracil (3,200 mg/m² over 48 hours). Patients in the United 
States have been shown to have greater toxicity with fluorouracil. The dose of fluorouracil (2,400 mg/m² over 46 hours) is a starting dose consistent with the dose 
recommended in FOLFOX or FOLFIRI and should be strongly considered for U.S. patients.
h Oncology Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities.
i If disease response, consider discontinuing checkpoint inhibitor after 2 years of treatment.
j If no previous treatment with a checkpoint inhibitor. Nivolumab + ipilimumab may be considered as subsequent therapy if checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy was 
previously received. 
k Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy 
subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV 
nivolumab.
l Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph 
has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab.
m If patient is unable to tolerate EGFR inhibitor due to toxicity, single-agent adagrasib or sotorasib can be considered.
n If no previous treatment with HER2 inhibitor.
o Some activity was seen after a previous HER2-targeted regimen. May not be indicated in patients with underlying lung issues due to lung toxicity (3.5% report of drug-
related deaths from interstitial lung disease on the DESTINY-CRC01 trial).
p On the TRIDENT-1 trial, repotrectinib showed activity in both NTRK TKI-naïve and NTRK TKI-pretreated patients.
q Patients with BRAF mutations other than V600E may be considered for anti-EGFR therapy.
r Cetuximab or panitumumab are recommended in combination with irinotecan-based therapy or as single-agent therapy for patients who cannot tolerate irinotecan.
APP-D
3 OF 11

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURVIVORSHIP
content:
Surveillance  
• Surveillance recommendations can be found on APP-6 and APP-8.
• Long-term surveillance should be carefully managed with routine good 
medical care and monitoring, including cancer screening, routine health 
care, and preventive care.
• Routine CEA monitoring and routine CT scanning are not recommended 
beyond 5 years for high-risk disease. For low-risk disease, routine CEA 
monitoring and routine CT scanning are not recommended beyond 10 
years.
Survivorship Care Planning
The oncologist and primary care provider should have defined roles in the 
surveillance period, with roles communicated to the patient.1
• Develop survivorship care plan that includes:
Overall summary of treatment, including all surgeries, radiation 
treatments, and systemic therapy received.
Description of possible expected time to resolution of acute toxicities, 
long-term effects of treatment, and possible late sequelae of treatment.
Surveillance recommendations.
Delineation of appropriate timing of transfer of care with specific 
responsibilities identified for primary care physician and oncologist.
Health behavior recommendations.
Fertility counseling.
Management of Late/Long-Term Sequelae of Disease or Treatment2-6 
• For issues related to distress, pain, neuropathy, fatigue, or sexual 
dysfunction, see Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.
• For chronic diarrhea or incontinence:
Consider anti-diarrheal agents, bulk-forming agents, diet manipulation, 
pelvic floor rehabilitation, and protective undergarments.
• Management of an ostomy:
Consider participation in an ostomy support group or coordination of 
care with a health care provider specializing in ostomy care (ie, ostomy 
nurse).
Screen for distress around body changes (Oncology Guidelines for 
Distress Management) and precautions around involvement with 
physical activity (see page SPA-C in the Oncology Guidelines for 
Survivorship).
• For oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy:
Consider duloxetine for painful neuropathy only, not effective for 
numbness, tingling, or cold sensitivity.7
Consider non-pharmacologic therapies such as heat or acupuncture.
Pregabalin or gabapentin are not recommended.
Counseling Regarding Healthy Lifestyle and Wellness8  
(Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship)
• Undergo all age- and gender-appropriate cancer and preventive health 
screenings as per national guidelines.
• Maintain a healthy body weight throughout life.
• Adopt a physically active lifestyle (at least 30 minutes of moderate-
intensity activity on most days of the week). Activity recommendations 
may require modification based on treatment sequelae (ie, ostomy, 
neuropathy).
• Consume a healthy diet with emphasis on plant sources. Diet 
recommendations may be modified based on severity of bowel 
dysfunction.
• Drink alcohol sparingly, if at all.
• Receive smoking cessation counseling as appropriate (Oncology Guidelines 
for Smoking Cessation).
Additional health monitoring and immunizations should be performed 
as indicated under the care of a primary care physician. Survivors are 
encouraged to maintain a therapeutic relationship with a primary care 
physician throughout their lifetime.

American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM Staging Classification for Appendix Cancer 9th ed., 2022
Table 1. Definitions for T, N, M
T
Primary Tumor
TX
Primary tumor cannot be assessed
T0
No evidence of primary tumor
Tis
Carcinoma in situ (intramucosal carcinoma; invasion of 
the lamina propria or extension into but not through the 
muscularis mucosae)
Tis(LAMN)
Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm confined to the 
muscularis propria; Acellular mucin or mucinous epithelium 
may invade into the muscularis propria
T1 and T2 are not applicable to LAMN; Acellular mucin or 
mucinous epithelium that extends into the subserosa or 
serosa should be classified as T3 or T4a, respectively
T1
Tumor invades the submucosa (through the muscularis 
mucosa but not into the muscularis propria)
T2
Tumor invades the muscularis propria
T3
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the 
subserosa or the mesoappendix
T4
Tumor invades the visceral peritoneum, including acellular 
mucin or mucinous epithelium involving the serosa of 
the appendix or mesoappendix, and/or directly invades 
adjacent organs or structures
T4a
Tumor invades the visceral peritoneum, including acellular 
mucin or mucinous epithelium involving the serosa of the 
appendix or serosa of the mesoappendix
T4b
Tumor directly invades or adheres to adjacent organs or 
structures
N
Regional Lymph Nodes
NX
Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
N0
No tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s)
N1
Tumor involvement of one to three regional lymph nodes (tumor 
in lymph node measuring greater than or equal to 0.2 mm) or any 
number of tumor deposits is present with no tumor involvement in 
all identifiable lymph nodes
N1a
Tumor involvement of one regional lymph node
N1b
Tumor involvement of two or three regional lymph nodes
N1c
No tumor involvement of regional lymph nodes, but there are 
tumor deposits in the subserosa or mesentery
N2
Tumor involvement of four or more regional lymph nodes
M
Distant Metastasis
cM0
No distant metastasis
cM1
Distant metastasis
cM1c Metastasis to sites other than peritoneum
pM1
Microscopic confirmation of distant metastasis
pM1a Intraperitoneal acellular mucin, without identifiable tumor cells  
in the disseminated peritoneal mucinous deposits
pM1b Intraperitoneal metastasis only, including peritoneal mucinous 
deposits containing tumor cells
pM1c Microscopic confirmation of metastasis to sites other than 
peritoneum
Note: For specimens containing acellular mucin without identifiable 
tumor cells, efforts should be made to obtain additional tissue for 
thorough histologic examination to evaluate for cellularity.

Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-2
Overview
Appendiceal cancers (AC) and appendiceal neoplasms comprise a 
spectrum of rare tumors of the digestive system often found incidentally on 
surgical specimens following appendectomy for presumed acute 
appendicitis. Malignant tumors of the appendix are confirmed by 
pathologic review in 0.9% to 1.4% of all appendectomy specimens and 
0.4% of all confirmed gastrointestinal (GI) cancers.1,2 An epidemiologic 
study put the age-adjusted incidence of AC at 0.12 cases per 1,000,000 
people per year,3 although other studies have put incidence rates between 
0.63 to 0.97 per 100,000 people.4
Although primary appendiceal malignancies are still considered rare, 
several studies have pointed towards a recent increase in incidence 
rates.4-6 Between 2000 and 2016 the incidence rate increased by 232% in 
the United States, while rates of appendicectomies remained the same, 
suggesting that the rise in AC is not directly related to an increase in 
incidental tumors.5 In a study comparing trends in AC with right-sided 
colon cancers from 2004 to 2017, out of 387,867 patients with right-sided 
colon cancer (including appendiceal), approximately 5% had AC and the 
odds of AC, relative to right-sided colon cancer, increased over time, most 
markedly in patients between 40 to 49 years of age.7 A 2025 study 
comparing incidence rates of AC across different birth cohorts found that 
they more than tripled among the 1980 birth cohort and quadrupled among 
the 1985 birth cohort, when compared with the 1945 birth cohort.6 These 
increases were noted for all histologic tumor types.
Historically, diagnosing appendix cancer has been difficult due to non-
specific early onset of symptoms, lack of clinical or pathologic 
understanding leading to misdiagnosis, low incidence rates and small 
sample sizes resulting in low-quality data, and differentiating diagnosis of 
ovarian adnexal-derived tumors.8 Unfortunately, due to the rarity and 
heterogeneity of the disease, few clinical advancements have been made
in the last decade; although, it is well known that histologic subtype in 
addition to tumor grade and stage have been strongly attributed to 
variations in survival outcomes.9,10
The Oncology Guidelines Panel’s recommendations for management of appendiceal 
tumors were previously included within the Oncology Guidelines Clinical Practice 
Guidelines in Oncology (Oncology Guidelines®) for Colon Cancer, with few 
recommendations specific to AC. These Oncology Guidelines® for 
Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers represent the Oncology Guidelines Panel’s 
acknowledgement of AC as a unique entity with a spectrum of histologic 
subtypes requiring specific recommendations for treatment. Concurrent to 
this, there have been several large efforts to create independent 
guidelines, notably the Chicago Consensus Working Group and Peritoneal 
Surface Malignancies Consortium Group.11-13 The Oncology Guidelines begin 
with the clinical presentation of the patient and address diagnosis, 
histopathologic evaluation, pathologic staging, surgical management, 
systemic therapy, surveillance, management of recurrent and metastatic 
disease, and survivorship. Although these guidelines are believed to 
represent the optimal treatment strategy, participation in a clinical trial, 
when available, is especially encouraged for patients with AC based on 
the lack of clinical trial data on which to base treatment decisions.
Guidelines Update Methodology
The complete details of the Development and Update of the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines are available at www.Oncology Guidelines.
Literature Search Criteria
Prior to the update of the Oncology Guidelines for Appendiceal Neoplasms 
and Cancers, an electronic search of the PubMed database was 
performed to obtain key literature in the field of appendiceal neoplasms 
and cancers published within the previous decade, using the following 
search terms: appendiceal adenocarcinoma, appendiceal neoplasm,

MS-3
appendix cancer, pseudomyxoma peritonei, low-grade appendiceal 
mucinous neoplasms (LAMN), high-grade appendiceal mucinous 
neoplasms (HAMN). The PubMed database was chosen as it remains the 
most widely used resource for medical literature and indexes peer-
reviewed biomedical literature.14
The search results were narrowed by selecting studies in humans 
published in English. Results were confined to the following article types: 
Clinical Trial, Phase II; Clinical Trial, Phase III; Clinical Trial, Phase IV; 
Guideline; Practice Guideline; Randomized Controlled Trial; Meta-
Analysis; Systematic Reviews; and Validation Studies. The data from key 
PubMed articles as well as articles from additional sources deemed as 
relevant to these Guidelines as discussed by the Panel during the 
Guidelines update have been included in this version of the Discussion 
section. Recommendations for which high-level evidence is lacking are 
based on the Panel’s review of lower-level evidence and expert opinion.
Sensitive/Inclusive Language Usage
Oncology Guidelines strive to use language that advances the goals of 
equity, inclusion, and representation.15 Oncology Guidelines endeavor to use 
language that is person-first; not stigmatizing; anti-racist, anti-classist, anti-
misogynist, anti-ageist, anti-ableist, and anti-weight-biased; and inclusive 
of individuals of all sexual orientations and gender identities. Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines incorporate non-gendered language, instead focusing on 
organ-specific recommendations. This language is both more accurate 
and more inclusive and can help fully address the needs of individuals of 
all sexual orientations and gender identities. Oncology Guidelines will 
continue to use the terms men, women, female, and male when citing 
statistics, recommendations, or data from organizations or sources that do 
not use inclusive terms. Most studies do not report how sex and gender 
data are collected and use these terms interchangeably or inconsistently. 
If sources do not differentiate gender from sex assigned at birth or organs
present, the information is presumed to predominantly represent cisgender 
individuals. Oncology Guidelines encourages researchers to collect more specific data in 
future studies and organizations to use more inclusive and accurate 
language in their future analyses.
Risk Assessment
According to the National Cancer Database (NCDB), out of 11,799 people 
diagnosed with AC, 55.3% were female, 77.9% were non-Hispanic white, 
9.5% were non-Hispanic Black, 6.0% were Hispanic, and 2.5% were 
Asian/Pacific Islanders.16 In 2021, a study compared trends in histologic 
subtype, tumor grade, stage, sex, and ethnicity in 8851 individuals 
diagnosed with AC.10 The individuals in this study were in the United 
States between years 2007 to 2016 and were at least 20 years of age at 
primary AC diagnosis. Out of the total number of participants, 30.8% were 
diagnosed before 50 years of age. Patients diagnosed with early-onset AC 
(≤50 years of age) were 82% more likely to be Hispanic, American Indian, 
or Alaskan Native compared to patients diagnosed at >50 years of age.10 
In contrast, the overall incidence rate in those who were white and Black 
generally was the same across all ages.17,18 Patients with early-onset AC 
were also more likely to be diagnosed with neuroendocrine tumors (NETs) 
in addition to early-onset tumors being 45% to 61% less likely to be 
pathologically diagnosed as stage (III–IV) high-grade tumors.5,10 Data also 
suggest that intestinal NETs, including those of the appendix, have a 
higher incidence rate in individuals effected by obesity (body mass index 
[BMI]:30+ kg/m2).19
Clinical Presentation & Workup
Patients may present with symptoms resembling appendicitis, which 
include but are not limited to: abdominal pain in the right lower quadrant, 
vomiting, change in bowel habits, intestinal obstruction, compression of 
the ureters, and nausea.17,20-22 There should be increased suspicion of an 
appendiceal neoplasm if the patient is >50 years of age with a family

MS-4
history of inflammatory bowel disease, colon cancer, and/or unexplained 
anemia.23 Additionally, if appendicitis is treated nonoperatively (typically 
with antibiotics), repeat interval imaging is crucial to ensure that the 
imaging findings resolve. Lack of resolution may suggest an appendiceal 
malignancy.
Diagnosing neoplasms of the appendix is challenging and symptoms may 
overlap with colon cancer, gynecologic cancers, or other abdominal 
pathologies. Diagnostic tests at initial presentation include CT imaging, 
diagnostic laparoscopy or image-guided biopsy, and in some cases 
surgery, in addition to a thorough medical history and physical 
examination, including complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive 
metabolic panel (CMP).24 An appendix >15 mm on a CT or MRI with an 
irregular or thickened wall is suggestive of AC.25 Non-specific abdominal 
bloating/distention and/or postprandial discomfort may also be observed 
with mucinous peritoneal involvement. Colonoscopy is recommended to 
rule out other primary lesions, although it may be done concurrently with 
other diagnostic tests and should not delay treatment.25,26 A negative 
colonoscopy does not rule out an appendix primary lesion, as distal 
primaries are typically unable to be seen luminally. If the diagnosis is 
reached postoperatively, a thorough review of operative notes and 
pathology slides is encouraged in order to rule out more aggressive 
disease and other findings such as presence of perforation, ascites, extent 
of peritoneal spread, and/or the presence of extracellular mucin.
Several studies have investigated the use of tumor markers, including 
carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), CA 19-9, and CA-125, for 
prognostication of appendiceal neoplasms and cancers. In one such 
study, a multivariate analysis concluded that when CA 19-9 was elevated 
preoperatively, progression-free survival (PFS) was unfavorable. 
Additionally, when CEA was elevated preoperatively, overall survival (OS) 
was also unfavorable.27 CA-125 level was inconclusive in this study;
however, other studies suggest normal serum levels of CA-125 and CA 
19-9 correspond to an increase in survival and a decrease in recurrence.28 
Similarly, a retrospective cohort study of 1338 patients with appendiceal 
adenocarcinoma (AA) found that elevations of CEA, CA 19-9, or CA-125 
were associated with worse 5-year survival, and patients with highly 
elevated (top 10th percentile) markers had markedly worse survival 
outcomes.29 Therefore, it is recommended to measure CEA, CA 19-9, and 
CA-125 marker levels as part of initial workup.
Upon confirmation of appendiceal neoplasms or cancer, fertility risk 
discussion and counseling are recommended in appropriate patients, 
including those with mucinous carcinoma peritonei (MCP) who wish to 
preserve fertility.30

header: Pathology & Molecular Classification
content:
Appendiceal neoplasms are a heterogenous group of tumors with 
controversial historic and confusing nomenclature. Unified terminology is 
key in obtaining correct classification and subsequent clinical management 
for appendiceal tumors with and without peritoneal involvement. The 
following pathologic classifications are based on the 9th edition of the 
AJCC and 5th edition of the WHO Classification of Digestive System 
Tumors.11-13,31-34
Appendiceal Serrated Lesions and Polyps
Serrated epithelial lesions arising in the appendix can be reactive or 
neoplastic. The histologic diagnosis of reactive mucosal hyperplasia refers 
to flat, serrated surface epithelium lacking cytologic features of dysplasia 
often arising in the post-inflammatory repair setting after acute 
appendicitis, appendiceal diverticular disease, or after an interval 
appendectomy.32 Nomenclature for appendiceal serrated lesions and 
polyps is emerging and varies in the literature. The WHO 5th edition 
classifies serrated entities as mucosal hyperplasia, hyperplastic polyp,

MS-5
serrated lesion with dysplasia, and serrated lesion without dysplasia. Non-
serrated dysplastic lesions, similar to their colorectal counterparts, can 
also arise within the appendix. However, the clinical significance of the 
categories is the same in that dysplastic lesions have a risk of progression 
to invasive adenocarcinoma and non-dysplastic lesions do not. Dysplastic 
serrated lesions in the appendix may be distinct from colorectal 
counterparts in the absence of BRAF mutations and presence of KRAS 
and GNAS mutations.32,35-37
Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasms
Current preferred terminology for appendiceal mucinous neoplasms is low-
grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN) and high-grade 
appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (HAMN).32,34 Historic terminology no 
longer recommended includes: mucinous tumor of uncertain malignant 
potential, borderline tumor of the appendix, and mucinous 
cystadenocarcinoma of the appendix.
By definition, LAMN and HAMN refer to appendiceal mucinous neoplasms 
that do not demonstrate an infiltrative growth pattern or desmoplasia. 
Instead both are characterized by dysplastic mucinous epithelium, 
accompanied by effacement of the lamina propria, loss of lymphoid tissue, 
and presence of submucosal fibrosis. Characteristic cytologic and 
architectural features separate these two entities. LAMN demonstrates tall 
mucinous epithelial cells with mild cytologic atypia in a villous or flat 
arrangement. HAMN retains similar features as LAMN but shows 
progression to a more aggressive neoplasm with the acquisition of 
unequivocal high-grade cytologic features and more complex architecture. 
Both LAMN and HAMN exhibit a rounded, pushing pattern of invasion and 
do not demonstrate an infiltrative growth pattern or associated 
desmoplastic response that defines invasive adenocarcinoma. Mucin 
pools, acellular or with dysplastic mucinous epithelium, are common in 
both neoplasms and may extend into the serosa and beyond. LAMN
confined to the submucosa or muscularis propria, including acellular or 
mucinous epithelium, is staged as carcinoma in situ (pTis). pT1 and pT2 
are not applicable. A tumor that extends into subserosa is staged as pT3. 
pT4 refers to a tumor that perforates the appendix with extension of either 
acellular mucin or mucin with neoplastic cells. pT4 is further subdivided 
into invasion into the visceral peritoneum involving serosa of the appendix 
or mesoappendix (pT4a) and direct invasion or adherence to adjacent 
organs or structures that is not luminal or mural extension (T4b). HAMN is 
staged as mucinous adenocarcinoma based on recent evidence of 
biologic potential. However, robust data are rare and limited.11-13,34,35,38-40 
Mucin that involves peritoneal surfaces beyond the appendix are staged 
as pM1a if the mucin is acellular and pM1b if the mucin contains 
neoplastic epithelial cells.
Molecular studies performed on LAMN/HAMNs are limited and also 
fraught with challenges extending from confusing historical nomenclature. 
However, recent analyses incorporating expert GI pathology review have 
been published. Findings on small- and large-scale next-generation 
sequencing (NGS) panels show the vast majority of LAMNs and HAMNs 
harbor KRAS and GNAS mutations.41-43 Activating BRAF mutations are 
rarely seen and when discovered are often class II and not BRAF V600E. 
TP53 mutations were not identified in LAMNs in these careful studies, but 
were identified in a percentage of HAMNs as well as mucinous 
adenocarcinomas.43-46 Neither mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) nor 
microsatellite instability (MSI) have been identified in LAMNs or HAMNs, 
outside of patients with suspicion of Lynch syndrome, reviewed by expert 
GI pathologists.47
Peritoneal metastases from LAMN or HAMN may have a discordant, more 
aggressive disease histology and should be treated accordingly.

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-6
Appendiceal Adenocarcinoma (AA)
Outside of appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMN, HAMN), primary 
ACs are rare. AA demonstrate a variety of histologic patterns similar to 
those arising in the colorectum. They can be generally divided into 
mucinous and non-mucinous adenocarcinomas. Mucinous AAs can be 
challenging to distinguish from mucinous neoplasms (LAMN and HAMN). 
However, AA is highly cellular, has high-grade nuclear features, and must 
show an infiltrative growth pattern with a desmoplastic response.32,34,48,49 
Mucinous adenocarcinoma comprise >50% extracellular mucin, with 
floating strips, glands, or clusters of neoplastic mucinous epithelial cells. 
Signet ring cell adenocarcinoma refers to a tumor with >50% consisting of 
signet ring cells, similar to signet ring cell carcinoma arising elsewhere 
within the GI tract. UC is an aggressive subtype of carcinoma 
characterized by lack of morphologic, immunohistochemical, and 
molecular evidence of differentiation beyond that of epithelial origin.
AJCC grading of mucinous adenocarcinomas and non-mucinous 
adenocarcinomas involves a similar three-tier grading scheme. However, 
cytologic and architectural criteria are different. For mucinous 
adenocarcinoma, grade 1 (well-differentiated) tumors demonstrate low-
grade cytologic atypia and lack signet ring cells. Grade 2 (moderately 
differentiated) tumors show a mix of low-grade and high-grade cytologic 
atypia but lack a signet ring cell component. Grade 3 (poorly differentiated) 
tumors exhibit high-grade cytologic atypia and the presence of a signet 
ring cell component, usually >10%. Non mucinous adenocarcinomas are 
graded similar to other locations in the colorectum: G1 (well differentiated, 
>95% gland formation), G2 (moderately differentiated, 50%–95% gland 
formation), and G3 (poorly differentiated, <50% gland formation).
Staging is similar for both mucinous adenocarcinoma and non-mucinous 
adenocarcinoma: pT1 invasion into the submucosa; pT2 into the 
muscularis propria; pT3 through the muscularis propria and into the
subserosa or mesoappendix; pT4a through the visceral peritoneum, 
including acellular mucin or mucinous epithelium involving serosa; and 
T4b direct invasion or adherence to adjacent organs or structures. Lymph 
node (pN) categories are also similar: pN1a tumor involvement in one 
regional lymph node; pN1b tumor involvement in two or three positive 
regional lymph nodes; pN1c with no tumor involvement in lymph nodes but 
tumor deposits are identified in the subserosa or mesentery; and pN2 
tumor involving ≥4 regional lymph nodes.
AA rarely demonstrates dMMR or MSI-high (MSI-H), unlike 
adenocarcinomas of the colorectum.50 Several studies have sought to 
develop a molecular profile of appendiceal neoplasms to guide clinical 
decision-making and identify similarities and differences from colon 
cancer. However, early studies grouped LAMN/HAMN in with other 
appendiceal neoplasms creating a challenge in interpreting prognostic 
significance.51,52 A recent molecular study sequenced AA from 273 
patients following a consensus diagnosis provided by expert GI 
pathologists. The tumors included were mucinous AA, goblet cell 
adenocarcinoma (GCA), and colonic-type (non-mucinous) 
adenocarcinoma. All tumors were MMR proficient (pMMR). In addition, 
within the mucinous adenocarcinoma group, the authors identified four 
distinct molecular subtypes: a clinically indolent RAS-mutant predominant 
subtype (RAS-mutant/GNAS-wild-type/all TP53-wild-type); a 
chemotherapy-resistant GNAS-mutant predominant subtype (GNAS-
mutant/RAS-wild-type or GNAS-mutant/RAS-mutant, and all TP53-wild-
type); an aggressive, highly aneuploid TP53-mutant predominant subtype 
(all TP53 mutant); and a triple-negative subtype (RAS/GNAS/TP53-wild-
type).53 These molecular subtypes and associated clinical outcomes were 
similar in both the colonic type, non-mucinous adenocarcinoma and GCA 
groups, providing support for incorporating molecular signatures into the 
clinical management of AA.53

Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-7
A study of 266 patients with metastatic AA revealed similar frequently 
mutated genes in the following descending order: RAS (KRAS or NRAS), 
GNAS, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA and APC. Well- and moderately 
differentiated AA were enriched for RAS and GNAS compared to poorly 
differentiated tumors and had a lower rate of TP53 mutations. In addition, 
this study evaluated the concordance between circulating tumor DNA 
(ctDNA) and tissue-based mutational profiling. Compared to colorectal 
cancer (CRC), AA was less likely to shed ctDNA with only 38% of patients 
found to have detectable ctDNA.54 Of the tumors with detectable ctDNA, 
TP53 and KRAS were the most commonly mutated genes, similar to 
tissue-based studies on higher-grade AA. Separate studies have reported 
similar discordant results between tissue-based and ctDNA assays 
highlighting the challenges of utilizing ctDNA to inform prognosis and 
therapy.55
Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma
Appendiceal GCAs were previously classified as NETs but are now 
recognized as a distinct clinicopathologic entity with biologic potential 
similar to adenocarcinomas.32 Historic terminology no longer 
recommended includes goblet cell carcinoid, goblet cell type 
adenocarcinoid, mucinous carcinoid, and crypt cell carcinoma.35
By definition, GCAs must demonstrate at least some component of 
characteristic low-grade morphologic features, which include tubules of 
goblet-like cells and occasional evidence of endocrine or Paneth cell 
differentiation. Nuclear cytologic atypia is mild. The tumor cells may be 
arranged in groups of cells without lumina or as fused tubules. 
Extracellular mucin is often identified and can sometimes be abundant. 
High-grade morphology is defined by high-grade nuclear cytologic atypia, 
signet ring cell morphology, and abundant mitotic activity. Necrosis may 
be present. Tumor cells invade as single cells, in sheets, or demonstrate 
complex anastomosing tubules. Perineural invasion is commonly seen in
both low-grade and high-grade GCA. However, lymphovascular invasion is 
more commonly associated with high-grade morphology. GCAs are 
graded according to a unique system: Grade 1: >75% low-grade growth 
pattern; Grade 2: 50% to 75% low-grade growth pattern; and Grade 3: 
>50% high-grade growth pattern.32 If >50% of the neoplastic cell 
population is comprised of signet ring cell morphology, and a low-grade 
component is not identified, the tumor is categorized as a signet ring cell 
adenocarcinoma. GCAs are staged the same as other AA.32,33
Similar to other appendiceal neoplasms, GCA are predominately pMMR.56 
Sequencing studies show GCA are biologically distinct from colorectal 
adenocarcinoma in the lack of APC, KRAS, and TP53 mutations.56,57 
Instead, GCA more often harbor alterations in chromatin remodeling 
complex pathway genes including ARID1A, ARID2, KDM6A, 
and KMT2D (MLL2)] or WNT signaling-associated genes 
USP9X, NOTCH1, CTNNA1, CTNNB1, and TRRAP.57,58
Mucinous Carcinoma Peritonei (Pseudomyxoma Peritonei)
Pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP) is a term used to describe the clinical 
presentation of mucinous tumor deposits in the peritoneum also described 
as diffuse progressive mucinous ascites.13,59 The WHO uses PMP to 
describe all peritoneal mucinous tumors regardless of grade.32 The current 
histologic terminology for mucinous neoplasms in the peritoneum is 
MCP.11-13,32,33 Historic terminology no longer recommended includes: 
disseminated peritoneal adenomucinosis and peritoneal mucinous 
carcinomatosis with or without signet ring cells.
It is now understood that PMP is most often secondary to LAMN and 
HAMN.13 Nomenclature, as well as pathologic grade and stage, for entities 
within the peritoneum has evolved in an attempt to mirror biologic potential 
of mucinous neoplasms within the appendix.11-13,32,33,60 Accordingly, 
appendiceal neoplasms with intraperitoneal acellular mucin are staged as

MS-8
pM1a, mucinous deposits containing tumor cells as pM1b, and metastasis 
to sites other than the peritoneum as pM1c. The AJCC provides a grading 
scheme for the neoplastic cells within peritoneal mucin. Grade 1 
MCP/PMP (well-differentiated) is defined as harboring low-grade cytologic 
atypia and absence of signet ring cells. Grade 2 MCP/PMP (moderately 
differentiated) shows a mix of low-grade and high-grade cytologic atypia 
and absence of signet ring cells. Grade 3 MCP/PMP (poorly differentiated) 
refers to neoplastic cells with high-grade cytologic atypia usually with a 
signet ring cell component.11-13,32,33,60  For patients with previous treatment, 
a peritoneal regression grading score has been proposed although only 
validated in combination with peritoneal cytology.13,61,62
Recent studies with careful histologic review focused on molecular 
alterations in peritoneal disease only (PMP).43 In one study, alterations in 
TP53, SMAD4, ATM, CDKN2A, and mTOR pathway genes were 
associated with older mean age, higher AJCC/WHO histologic grade, 
lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, regional lymph node 
metastasis, lower mean PCI, and lower OS rates. Several other genomic 
studies focused on cellular peritoneal mucin demonstrated a higher 
percentage of TP53 mutations along with alterations in SMAD4 and 
ARID1A.39,46 Independently, TP53 mutations have been associated with a 
worse prognosis in these patients.63
Appendiceal Neuroendocrine Neoplasms
See the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors 
(available at www.Oncology Guidelines) for more information.
Targeted Therapy and Appendiceal Neoplasms
Molecular alterations with associated targeted therapy options have been 
reported in a subset of patients with AA, such as KRAS G12C, PIK3CA, 
and NTRK1 fusions. However, several large genomic studies have shown 
AA is molecularly distinct from colorectal adenocarcinoma.53-55,64 For
example, BRAF V600E is rarely observed and the incidence of MSI-H or 
dMMR in all subtypes of primary and disseminated appendiceal neoplasm 
is very low.55,65 Recent large genomic studies have reported tumor 
mutational burden (TMB) and MSI status in patients with AA. In one study 
of all stage AA, AA had a low TMB compared to rectal and colon cancers 
and only 1.3% of AA were MSI-H.65,66 See Palliative Systemic Therapy for 
Advanced or Metastatic Disease, below, for more information on targeted 
therapy options for AA.
Imaging
Computed Tomography (CT) Scan
CT is the imagining modality of choice due to its high diagnostic accuracy, 
helping to differentiate between appendicitis and appendiceal neoplasms 
as well as extent of disease in cases of malignancy.67,68 CT imaging of 
acute appendicitis shows appendiceal thickening defined as the outer 
diameter measuring >6 mm and inflammatory thickening of the 
appendiceal wall measuring ≥3 mm, in addition to mural 
hyperenhancement.69 Similarly, NETs of the appendix appear on CT with 
the characteristics of appendicitis but with the addition of a small mass 
located toward the tip of the appendix. These findings mimic the 
characteristics of appendicoliths, making NET hard to definitively 
diagnosis before pathology review. Non-mucinous neoplasm of the 
appendix will also resemble appendicitis but with the presence of a soft 
tissue mass either obstructing the appendiceal lumen or infiltrating the 
appendiceal wall. Other imaging findings may include direct infiltration to 
surrounding organs, lymphadenopathy, and possible metastasis.69 CT 
images of mucinous neoplasms are not usually consistent with 
appendicitis unless infection and inflammation arise. These findings are 
due in part to the large quantities of mucin being secreted into the lumen 
causing luminal distention without significant inflammation.20,69 Other 
clinical manifestations of mucinous neoplasms may include a palpable

MS-9
mass; PMP causing an increase in abdominal girth; torsion and right 
ureteral obstruction; and intussusception.69
FDG-PET/CT
PET using 18fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) is not indicated in the workup for 
appendiceal neoplasms or cancers, mainly due to low sensitivities in both 
low-grade and high-grade lesions, with or without concomitant CT. In one 
study, the lower sensitivity for detecting high-grade and low-grade lesions 
with PET alone compared to PET/CT was not statistically significant.70 
Importantly, CT was the factor that improved imaging of these cases, and 
the authors concluded that FDG-PET should not be used for patients with 
appendiceal lesions. Patients with confirmed or suspected appendiceal 
neoplasms may be advised to undergo FDG-PET scans to aid in the 
detection of potential metastases in the liver, and to help establish the 
peritoneal cancer index (PCI), a tool used for staging the extent of 
peritoneal seeding.28,71,72
Imaging for patients with neuroendocrine neoplasms may include contrast-
enhanced CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis and view of the liver, the most 
frequent metastatic site. Somatostatin receptor imaging with 68Ga-
DOTATATE PET/CT should not be used routinely for diagnostic workup 
since contrast-enhanced CT is highly sensitive, but may be considered for 
neuroendocrine neoplasms, as discussed in the Oncology Guidelines for 
Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
MRI
MRI can be considered as an imaging option if CT findings in the setting of 
recurrent disease are normal, but disease presence is suspected. CT may 
not be feasible in several settings, warranting MRI of the abdomen to 
determine the extent of extraperitoneal disease.71,73 Preoperative use of 
MRI has demonstrated value for staging of HAMN and LAMN, regardless 
of CT accessibility.70,74
Diagnostic Laparoscopy
Following initial recommended imaging, diagnostic laparoscopy is an 
option to allow surgeons to obtain further information about the organs 
and adjacent regions that may be affected by lesions. If no metastases 
or peritoneal disease are found by imaging analyses, laparoscopy is 
indicated to be performed by a trained surgical oncologist. Diagnostic 
laparoscopy may help estimate the likelihood of complete cytoreduction 
as well as obtain tissue samples.75 Furthermore, since nodal disease has 
been shown to predict recurrence in patients with nonmetastatic AA, 
workup including diagnostic laparoscopy can be helpful for 
prognostication by identifying positive lymph nodes.76 A study of 685 
patients who underwent surgery for appendiceal neoplasms revealed 
that 11% of the individuals underwent diagnostic laparoscopy before 
surgery.77 Diagnostic laparoscopy was utilized to assess disease burden 
and further support the findings from imaging and staging of patients with 
high-risk histology, which they conclude could help avoid unnecessary 
laparotomy in many cases.
Image-Guided Biopsy
As part of diagnostic workup, image-guided biopsy may be considered 
as an alternative to diagnostic laparoscopy in select situations. Image-
guided biopsy is preferred for AC with possible extraperitoneal disease 
with significant ascites or for peritoneal-only disease where a non-
surgical approach is preferred and lesions may be safely accessible 
percutaneously. If lesions are inaccessible, surgical biopsy may be 
needed for diagnostic confirmation.
While FDG-PET/CT is regarded as one of the more advantageous 
options for biopsy since it can clearly identify hypermetabolic regions of 
target tissues, it is seldom performed since the lack of real-time imaging 
during sampling limits the denotation of the FDG-active area.78 Similarly,

MS-10
PET/CT is less useful in cases where malignant cells exhibit 
hypometabolic activity, leading to evasion of detection.79
Surgical Resection
Surgical resection for appendiceal neoplasms and cancers is done to 
prevent progression and recurrence of localized disease or to remove 
peritoneal deposits for metastatic disease. For lower-risk localized 
disease, appendectomy is routinely performed to remove tumor and 
alleviate inflammation of the lower GI tract and potential rupture, which 
could lead to peritoneal spread, peritonitis, and/or other inflammatory 
processes. Higher-risk histologies may require right hemicolectomy 
(RHC) for resection of localized disease. For metastatic disease to the 
peritoneum, cytoreductive surgery (CRS), with or without intraperitoneal 
chemotherapy (IPCT), is generally the preferred surgical option if 
complete resection of peritoneal disease is possible. Effective surgical 
options are available on a case-by-case basis, and specific techniques 
are discussed in more detail in the following sections. This is also 
discussed in greater detail, including assessment of the PCI score, in the 
later section on CRS/IPCT.
Appendectomy
Appendectomy is a well-refined surgical procedure that is commonly 
performed to treat appendicitis. An appendectomy involves the removal of 
the whole vermiform appendix by either open or laparoscopic surgery. 
These methods have utility in cancer care since removing an appendix 
with a localized appendiceal neoplasm has a strong chance of curing the 
disease.80 Moreover, appendiceal lesions are known to present as 
appendicitis since the symptoms patients experience from either condition 
can be similar. Studies have reported that appendiceal neoplasms can be 
found during (visually) and after (upon pathologic examination) an 
appendectomy for appendicitis or other GI tests.81,82 A retrospective study 
of patients who underwent appendectomies found that 2.1% of all patients
had neoplasms, with the majority being NETs.83 A different retrospective 
review revealed 0.4% patients who underwent appendectomies had 
carcinoid appendiceal tumors.84 In both of these studies, the patients 
received surgery for appendicitis. Further, 9 patients of 3071 with 
appendicitis were found to have appendiceal lesions, of which two cases 
were low-grade mucinous tumors.81 These nine patients were successfully 
treated with methods including laparoscopic appendectomy and partial 
cecectomy. Appendectomy successfully treated HAMN and LAMN, but 
additional examinations may be required to be certain an invasive 
adenocarcinoma is not present.75
The incidence of appendiceal neoplasms may be markedly higher with 
complicated appendicitis. A review of 387 patients with complicated 
appendicitis reported that 16.1% were found to have appendiceal 
neoplasms or cancers following surgery.85 The most common tumor type 
was mucinous neoplasm (54%), although NET (19%) and AA (13%) were 
also observed. These findings underscore the risks of non-operative or 
medical management of complicated appendicitis and suggest that 
radiologic and clinical predictors should be considered to guide treatment.
A study of 138 patients across 14 medical centers at a follow-up of 13 
years compared the impact of different resection modalities.86 Of the 138 
patients with appendiceal mucinous neoplasms, 70 had appendectomies, 
26 had cecectomies, and 37 had RHC. They found that patients who 
underwent RHC or appendectomy had similar OS and disease-specific 
survival (DSS) at 5 years post-surgery. The more extensive surgical 
techniques (eg, cecectomy, RHC) offered no advantage in these metrics 
for those patients with localized low-grade neoplasms.
Laparoscopic appendectomy has become widely popular for patients 
with appendicitis, or cancer presenting as appendicitis. Compared to 
open appendectomy, this method is associated with decreases in 
infection rates, recovery times, lengths of hospital stay, and mortality

rates.81,87,88 Open appendectomy can be considered in cases where 
laparoscopy is not feasible, the patient is hemodynamically unstable, or 
when equipment or expertise is absent in a clinical setting.89,90
Cecal Cuff Resection
Surgical intervention of appendiceal tumors that reside in the 
appendiceal orifice, the part of the appendix that opens into the cecum, 
presents a challenge for surgeons since the location requires careful 
consideration of surrounding structures while maintaining healthy 
margins. A cecal cuff resection involves surgical removal of the whole 
appendix along with a small portion of the cecum, without compromising 
the ileocecal valve.91 A case report of a patient with a sessile serrated 
lesion discovered during colonoscopy found that the combination of a 
standard laparoscopic appendectomy and cecal cuff resection led to a 
successful patient outcome while preserving most of the healthy cecal 
tissue.92 A cecal cuff resection may be considered for LAMN/HAMN that 
was resected via appendectomy with positive margins. If achieving a 
negative-margin resection with complete cytoreduction would result in 
narrowing of the ileocecal valve, ileocecectomy (as opposed to RHC) is 
the preferred option to minimize exposure of the retroperitoneum and the 
extent of colonic resection, given that lymph node harvest is not required.
Right Hemicolectomy (RHC)
Patients with higher-risk appendiceal cancers, such as with AA, GCA, or 
NET often require RHC, a procedure involving the removal of the right 
side of the colon and the entire appendix, rather than an 
appendectomy.75,93,94
RHC and appendectomy have been historically compared for their 
effectiveness in decreasing progressive disease and increasing OS. A 
small study of 36 patients with AA helped solidify the notion that curative 
resection survival rates vary widely based on the histologic type, T stage,
and tumor grade (differentiation status).95 Some reports conclude that 
RHC improved 1-, 3-, and 5-year survival rates in patients with 
appendiceal neoplasms when compared to appendectomy, especially for 
poorly or moderately differentiated stage 2 lesions.96,97 Another 
multicenter retrospective analysis of 168 patients with T1 AA compared 
the OS rates after undergoing either appendectomy or RHC.98 The 1-, 3-, 
and 5-year postoperative OS rates were 87.4%, 84.8%, and 71.8% for 
the appendectomy group and 95.2%, 91.4%, and 82.8% for the RHC 
group, yielding no significant differences between the two surgical 
modalities. A report comparing the histology and size of appendiceal 
tumors and lymph node involvement found that in patients with well-
differentiated NETs, who may have a higher rate of nodal metastases in 
larger tumors (11–20 mm), favorable outcomes are not further improved 
by RHC.99
A study involving 501 patients diagnosed with epithelial appendiceal 
lesions with peritoneal seeding found no survival advantage in patients 
treated with RHC when compared to appendectomy.100 The study 
concluded that RHC can be avoided in mucinous appendiceal tumors 
with peritoneal seeding unless there is substantial metastatic 
involvement documented by biopsy or an inadequate resection margin. 
Again, these studies can be hard to interpret given the mixture of low- 
and high-risk disease.
Based on these results, the Oncology Guidelines Panel recommends that 
appendectomy alone is sufficient for LAMN/HAMN localized to the 
appendix and completely resected with negative margins. In cases 
where there are concerns regarding margin status, cecal cuff resection 
with or without ileocecectomy may also be sufficient, depending on the 
extent of involvement. In rare cases, right hemicolectomy (RHC) may be 
required to achieve negative margin resection. However, the 
management of HAMN remains somewhat controversial, with some

MS-12
advocating for RHC. RHC is recommended for localized AA, GCA, or 
UC-not otherwise specified (NOS) histologies due to their higher risk of 
progression and recurrence. Adjuvant therapy should be strongly 
considered following RHC when nodal disease or perforation was noted 
during surgery or with high-risk features, as discussed in Systemic 
Therapy for Resectable Disease below.
Lymph Node Dissection
Lymph node removal during surgery for AC is common when the lesion 
has metastasized to regional lymph nodes, often warranting RHC even if 
the disease is confined to the appendix. Since resection of lymph nodes 
can allow for more precise staging, the prospect of curative outcomes 
may be greater.75 Lymph node dissection, defined as at least 12 lymph 
nodes removed and examined (extrapolated from Oncology Guidelines for 
Colon Cancer) is crucial for accurate staging, per the AJCC and College 
of American Pathologists.34,101 A major determinant of the necessity of 
lymph node dissection for AC is the histology of the tumor. Regionally 
advanced well-differentiated appendiceal tumors tend to have similar 
lymph node positivity compared to mixed-histology tumors, but the 
treatment of the two tumor histologies is often different. Generally, well-
differentiated tumors have lower rates of metastasis than those of mixed-
histology, but lymph node removal and more extensive surgery may 
benefit treatment of mixed-histology tumors.94
The need for a post-appendectomy RHC is highly dependent upon the 
extent of lymph node involvement for progressive disease. In 1731 
patients who had either well-differentiated or mixed-histology tumors, the 
type of surgery alone did not affect OS. However, tumor size was 
predictive of lymph node involvement, where higher rates of nodal 
metastases were seen in tumors between 11 to 20 mm for mixed-
histology than those ≤10 mm.99 Although lymph node involvement was 
similar between the two histologies, patients with well-differentiated
tumors were able to undergo an appendectomy alone or an RHC alone, 
with no difference in long-term OS.
There is controversy regarding the optimal number of lymph nodes to 
resect and evaluate for patients with AC. A mixed cohort of 1046 patients 
with mucinous or non-mucinous appendiceal tumors revealed a 
relationship between lymph nodes resected and node positivity for OS.102 
The finding that the rate of node-positive cancer increased with the 
number of regional nodes resected (up to 10 total nodes), was noted, but 
beyond 10 nodes resected the rate plateaued. Importantly, removing ≥12 
nodes, as per the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer, was associated 
with greater rates of OS when compared to removing <12, though there 
is no consensus for AA. This concept was investigated by Winicki et al 
where the number of lymph nodes harvested was evaluated for the 
potential impact on patient survival in mucinous and non-mucinous 
AA.103 Although the survival benefit was maximized when harvesting ≥15 
nodes for mucinous and 12 nodes for non-mucinous lesions, the main 
predictors of OS were the quality of lymph node removal surgery rather 
than surgery type (eg, appendectomy, RHC) and histology.

header: Treatment of Peritoneal Metastasis
content:
Peritoneal carcinomatosis (PC) is a neoplasm whose origin is from a 
primary tumor tissue (eg, appendix) that settles in the peritoneal space 
within the abdominal cavity. This spread can present a challenge for 
clinicians since it may be visually difficult to detect among surrounding 
structures and treatment can be limited owing to drug penetration and 
distribution issues. Therefore, the preferred treatment for appendiceal 
neoplasms and cancers with peritoneal spread is CRS, with or without 
IPCT, provided that a complete CRS is possible and the approach is 
recommended upon multidisciplinary discussion.
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:12:29 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-13
CRS/IPCT is recommended for eligible patients with either low- or high-
grade peritoneal deposits, although the specific considerations differ. For 
low-grade deposits or in cases where the pathology of the peritoneal 
deposits is unknown, observation may be considered in cases where 
CRS is not possible or mucin is acellular and localized to the 
periappendix area. A prospective study of 41 patients concluded that 
resected LAMN, including some with limited peritoneal spread, had a low 
(4.9%) risk of recurrence after appendectomy and local clearance of 
mucin.104 Another study of 125 patients with LAMN similarly concluded 
that overall recurrence risk for LAMN is low and appendectomy is 
sufficient for most cases, although those with acellular mucin confined to 
the right lower quadrant and a tumor size of <2 cm are at higher risk of 
recurrence.105 Contingent to an observation plan, LAMN histology must 
be confirmed by expert pathologic review.
For AA, GCA, or UC-NOS primaries or LAMN/HAMN with high-grade 
peritoneal metastases, neoadjuvant systemic therapy is recommended 
for up to 6 months before surgery. However, the data on whether 
neoadjuvant therapy improves outcomes in appendix cancer is mixed, as 
described below in Systemic Therapy for Resectable Disease.
Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS)/Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (IPCT)
CRS with concomitant IPCT is a treatment option consisting of a surgical 
procedure to remove all detectable signs of cancer from the abdominal 
space followed by chemotherapy applied directly to the peritoneum. CRS 
and IPCT have been used with reasonable success in patients who have 
PC stemming from primary AC. Compared to CRS alone, CRS + IPCT can 
extend patient OS and median PFS, especially for those where CRS was 
inadequate.106 For peritoneal metastases from AC, CRS and IPCT give 
select patients an increased chance of survival compared to systemic 
therapies.
Systemic therapies have particularly poor absorption in the peritoneum, 
explained by meager responses to intravenous chemotherapies delivered 
to treat PC before or after a CRS.107 Utilizing heat to increase drug 
penetration and effectiveness in the abdominal cavity, called hyperthermic 
intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC), is a commonly employed type of 
IPCT that has improved outcomes in patients with PC of various tissue 
origins.108,109 However, specific techniques used for IPCT can differ 
between institutions, and therefore, the Oncology Guidelines Panel does not recommend 
specific IPCT procedures or techniques.
A retrospective database report of 18,055 patients with AC compared the 
OS of patients receiving CRS + HIPEC versus surgery only and found 
that patients receiving CRS + HIPEC had a 5-year OS of 66%, compared 
to the 62% of those receiving surgery alone (P < .01), and a higher 5-
year OS for patients with well-differentiated cancers (eg, AA) when 
compared to all other treatment modalities (surgery + 
neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy, chemotherapy only, and neither 
surgery nor chemotherapy).110 Importantly, patients who received 
chemotherapy only had the poorest 5-year OS among all treatment 
groups, though this finding may have bias against those not deemed a 
candidate for surgery within this retrospective series.
Low-grade and well-differentiated AA histologies tend to be more 
amenable to this treatment combination, often leading to better patient 
outcomes and improved OS.106,111-113 The presence of acellular mucin in 
the abdomen, when mucinous content is localized to the periappendix 
area, is associated with better patient outcomes than neoplasms with 
cellular mucin since there are no malignant cells directly in the mucinous 
deposits.114 Accordingly, CRS and IPCT can be performed in patients 
with acellular mucinous appendiceal neoplasms with a reasonable 
potential of achieving a longer OS and DFS when compared to if those 
patients had cellular mucinous neoplasms. A study evaluating long-term
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:12:29 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
