Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-14
outcomes of patients with appendiceal neoplasms and acellular mucin 
(classified as M1a disease) compared to patients with mucin-containing 
tumor cells (classified as M1b disease) found that there are significantly 
different outcomes after CRS + HIPEC.115 At an overall median follow-up 
of 7.6 years, patients with M1a disease experienced no recurrences or 
metastases while 67% of patients with M1b disease experienced 
recurrence, with 33% of these patients dying during the study period. 
Patients with primary LAMNs or HAMNs who experience peritoneal 
disease should be assessed for mucinous deposits since potential 
procedural protocols are influenced by factors such as time of treatment, 
severity of disease, and the need for extensive care afterward.
Some studies have also investigated the role of CRS in high-grade AC. A 
study of 46 patients with mucinous and non-mucinous neoplasms 
originating from AC resulting in PC underwent CRS to improve disease 
management.116 Median DFS and OS after CRS were 20.5 months and 
56.4 months, respectively, with a 5-year OS of 45%. Variables involved 
in survival data in most studies for these methods include age, extent of 
CRS, previous chemotherapy, CA 19-9 levels, and tumor grade.116,117 A 
similar study looked at the outcome of 282 patients with PC originating 
from AA, with complete CRS achieved in 82% of patients involved.118 
Median OS in this study was 6.72 years with a 5-year OS probability of 
52.7%.
Laparoscopic CRS/IPCT, a less invasive version of CRS/IPCT, has been 
studied for its safety and effectiveness in patients with different 
appendiceal-derived histologies with some promising results.119,120 
Though this type of CRS/IPCT is not yet widely used, there is potential 
for decreasing morbidities that usually affect patients who undergo the 
standard CRS/IPCT procedures.
Laparoscopic Determination of CRS/IPCT Candidacy
PC originating from appendiceal neoplasms often warrants diagnostic 
laparoscopy or CT in order to help determine if a complete CRS/IPCT 
could potentially eradicate peritoneal disease. Laparoscopy is used 
before and during CRS to aid in staging and determination of candidacy 
and to limit the invasiveness of the procedure. Diagnostic laparoscopy 
may also be considered to help exclude patients from CRS/IPCT 
candidacy due to high-volume disease and/or unresectability.121 
Common findings that may exclude a patient from CRS/IPCT candidacy 
are extensive small bowel serosal involvement and/or high PCI values.
A single-center, retrospective study of 141 patients who underwent 
laparoscopies to determine whether patients were potential candidates 
for CRS/HIPEC found that of the 67 patients whose primary malignancy 
site was appendix, 48 of these patients who were deemed candidates 
underwent CRS/HIPEC.122 The study found an 82.8% positive predictive 
value for diagnostic laparoscopy, and concluded that the technique was 
safe, effective, and accurate for determining patient candidacy for CRS 
and HIPEC.
Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) and Completeness of Cytoreduction (CC)
The PCI, an assessment of extent of metastatic disease that aids in 
estimating the probability of complete cytoreduction, is generally 
determined preoperatively upon diagnostic imaging, but may also be 
determined during surgical exploration.123-126 The scale involves a visual 
quantification of the extent and location of peritoneal disease, scored as 
0 to 3 in each of the 13 regions of the abdominal cavity. Regions 0 
through 8 are oriented clockwise in the abdominal cavity, while regions 9 
through 12 represent the proximal and distal jejunum and the proximal 
and distal ileum. The potential of a successful CRS and IPCT can be 
predicted using PCI since factors such as tumor distribution in the 
abdominal and pelvic regions and lesion size scoring are standardized to
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:12:29 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 2.2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines© (Oncology Guidelines©), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers
MS-15
quantitatively measure carcinomatosis burden.124,127 A lower PCI score 
(10–20) tends to indicate a better prognosis and treatment outcome 
given the limited spread of disease whereas higher scores (20–30, >30) 
generally indicate extensive disease spread, which will limit therapeutic 
success of CRS and IPCT.124 Generally, a PCI score of ≤20 indicates 
suitability for CRS and IPCT in patients with PC originating from a GI 
primary malignancy.128 While PCI correlates with the ability to achieve a 
complete CRS, it is not associated with survival as long as a complete 
CRS can be achieved.129
Completeness of cytoreduction (CC) grades are standardly listed as 
follows: CC-0, no visible residual disease; CC-1, residual disease up to 
2.5 mm; CC-2, residual disease up to 2.5 cm; CC-3, residual disease 
>2.5 cm. The benefit and effectiveness of HIPEC hinges on the extent of 
CRS since complete microscopic tumor removal (CC-0) is ideal due to 
the limited penetration ability of IPCT.108,118 CC is a strong predictor of 
long-term patient outcome following CRS/IPCT since a higher CC score 
is associated with more residual nodules remaining in the affected 
area.130 A subset of patients with AA in a study evaluating long-term 
outcomes of patients with different mucinous cellular profiles revealed 
that patients with CC 0/1 CRS to control primary tumor pathologies 
exhibited significant differences in relapse-free survival (RFS) and OS 
based alone on the cellularity of mucinous deposits within the peritoneal 
cavity.115
CRS/IPCT for Recurrent Disease – Repeat Procedures
The need for more than a single CRS/IPCT procedure may be 
considered in patients who experience incomplete CRS or recurrent 
peritoneal metastases.111,131 An analysis of patients with peritoneal 
recurrences after initial treatment for LAMN who underwent either one or 
two CRS/HIPEC procedures revealed a significantly longer median OS 
for the patients who underwent two procedures versus one procedure
(227.1 vs. 54.5 months; P < .0001), along with an association of longer 
time between the two procedures and improved OS.132
A comparison of the outcomes of repeated CRS for patients with AC, 
featuring 102 patients who had repeat procedures due to recurrences, 
found that repeat CRS in higher volume peritoneal areas provided 
notable benefits, especially when the primary neoplasms were of 
appendiceal epithelial origin.131 Compared to 360 patients who only 
required a single procedure, the 102 patients who underwent multiple 
procedures had a significantly higher PCI score (24.0 vs. 29.5; P < .001). 
Notably, PCI scores were much lower for the second CRS/HIPEC 
procedure compared to the first in the repeat group (15 vs. 29.5; P < 
.001). Receiving multiple CRS/HIPEC procedures for patients with 
recurrence tends to lead to improved outcomes in this study, as well as 
in other previous studies with similar conclusions.131,133-136 If AC 
recurrence is seen in patients, CRS/IPCT is generally considered if 
symptomatic disease is present and/or low-grade tumors are found, 
preferably for someone with a longer disease-free interval.
As with any medical procedure, especially one as invasive and impactful 
as CRS/HIPEC, substantial risks exist that must be considered by the 
patient and clinician. In this study, a second procedure may compound a 
patient’s comorbidities. This is crucial to the success of an additional 
procedure as recovery is heavily impacted by treatment complications, 
requiring a delicate balance of symptom-driven necessity for surgery and 
patient desire to endure the surgery and recovery processes.111,132,137 For 
cases in which complete CRS is not possible, palliative CRS ± HIPEC 
can be considered an option to treat symptomatic disease, such as 
bowel obstruction.
Chemotherapeutic Agents Used in IPCT
Mitomycin C (MMC) is the mostly commonly used IPCT agent.118,138 
Oxaliplatin is an option that is less preferred and may be avoided if

patients are currently receiving or will receive systemic oxaliplatin in their 
treatment plan.139 A randomized 10-year follow-up study compared the 
OS and DFS of 121 patients with AC who underwent HIPEC for PC, 
receiving either MMC or oxaliplatin as the chemotherapeutic 
component.140 The 10-year OS for MMC and oxaliplatin groups were 
47.5% and 50.4% (P = .83) and the 10-year PFS values were 45.2% and 
50.4% (P = .95), respectively. Other studies have evaluated patient 
survival after CRS/HIPEC with different chemotherapy agents for 
treatment of AC and PMP. Five-year OS after MMC HIPEC are 
appreciable, with values ranging from 53% to 97%, depending on the 
origin of the tumor, dose of the chemotherapy, co-treatment with other 
chemotherapies, and duration of HIPEC treatment.136,141-146
Other IPCT regimens have been studied including 
oxaliplatin/fluorouracil/leucovorin (LV), cisplatin/MMC, cisplatin alone, 
and cisplatin/doxorubicin114,147; however, these are not currently 
recommended in the Oncology Guidelines for treatment of PC in AC.
Complications Associated with CRS/IPCT
Although the therapeutic value is compelling in eligible patients, CRS 
and IPCT come with complications that are both linked to morbidities and 
directly to the treatments themselves. Some of the most common 
complications linked to IPCT/HIPEC are hematologic toxicities, systemic 
infections, GI toxicities, and cardiovascular toxicities.148 A long-term 
follow-up study of patients with peritoneal metastases revealed that CRS 
and HIPEC treatments led to 32.4-month median survival values, but 
with a significant incidence of comorbidities.149 A study reporting on the 
association between risk factors and infection outcomes that featured 12 
patients with AC without PMP and 34 patients with PMP who underwent 
CRS and HIPEC showed that 43% of all patients involved experienced 
infections at the surgical site, respiratory tract, urinary tract, and 
sepsis.150 The critical finding in this study, the higher the burden, the
higher the chance of post-procedure complications, highlights the need 
for personalized treatment planning for patients. Some models have 
been developed to help predict severe postoperative complications 
following CRS/IPCT; these models may aid with patient selection and 
help providers anticipate and respond proactively to likely 
complications.151-153

header: Systemic Therapy for Resectable Disease
content:
Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy
While there is a body of literature investigating whether there is a benefit 
for neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to CRS/IPCT, the evidence has been 
mixed as to whether this approach offers a benefit, causes worse 
outcomes, or causes mixed outcomes.154 Elevated CEA levels may predict 
a survival benefit from neoadjuvant therapy.155
Several studies have shown a potential benefit to neoadjuvant therapy 
before CRS/IPCT.121,156-161 An analysis of prospectively collected data 
showed that 29% of 34 patients treated with preoperative systemic therapy 
had a complete or near-complete histologic response.157 Patients receiving 
preoperative therapy also had a lower PCI, required fewer resections, and 
achieved complete cytoreduction more frequently compared to patients 
who did not receive preoperative chemotherapy. Another study of 178 
patients with moderately or poorly differentiated AC similarly showed that 
47.1% of the patients who received neoadjuvant chemotherapy had a 
radiographic response and that stable or responsive disease was 
associated with improved OS.121 A third study reported a potential survival 
benefit with neoadjuvant systemic therapy for patients with signet ring cell 
histology (94%, 67%, and 22% survival at 1, 2, and 3 years with 
neoadjuvant systemic therapy compared to 43%, 14%, and 14% survival 
without neoadjuvant therapy; P = .028)158 A similar benefit was not seen 
among patients with high- or low-grade tumors without signet ring 
histology. Furthermore, a small retrospective review comparing a total

neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) approach prior to CRS/IPCT compared to a 
“sandwich” pre-/postoperative approach concluded that TNT showed 
improved OS and RFS.159
Other studies have shown either no benefit or even worse outcomes with 
neoadjuvant therapy prior to CRS/IPCT.117,162-168 A retrospective review of 
803 patients with AC showed no difference in OS, but worse RFS (14 vs. 
22 months; P = .007) with neoadjuvant chemotherapy before 
CRS/IPCT.162 Another retrospective analysis identified preoperative 
chemotherapy as a multivariate negative predictor of survival following 
CRS/HIPEC for both low-grade (P = .04) and high-grade (P = .0064) 
lesions.165 Similarly, a multicenter retrospective cohort study of patients 
with LAMN treated with CRS/HIPEC concluded that those who received 
perioperative systemic therapy had worse outcomes compared to those 
who did not.167 OS and RFS were 70.2 and 38.0 months with perioperative 
systemic therapy, compared to 104.3 and 84.9 months without (P < .001). 
As a fourth example, a retrospective review of 126 patients with high-
grade disease that was treated with CRS/HIPEC also concluded that 
neoadjuvant therapy was associated with decreases in OS and DFS and 
no differences in disease burden, cytoreduction, or recurrence patterns.168 
Five-year OS was 24.2% versus 5% (P = .017) and 5-year DFS was 
18.6% versus 5.7% (P = .048).
Based on these mixed data that show a potential benefit in select clinical 
situations, the Oncology Guidelines Panel recommends that neoadjuvant systemic 
therapy may be considered prior to CRS/IPCT for recurrence of high-risk 
disease with no previous CRS, and is a preferred approach for AA, GCA, 
or UC-NOS with metastatic disease in the peritoneum only. Perioperative 
therapy is not recommended for patients with LAMN or HAMN without 
high-grade peritoneal deposits based on data showing lack of benefit in 
this setting.
Adjuvant Chemotherapy
There are some studies on adjuvant therapy for appendiceal neoplasms 
and cancers that indicate that it may improve outcomes for certain patients 
following surgery for localized disease or CRS/IPCT, although data are 
limited and mixed.9,154,169-173
Two studies of the NCDB have investigated the role of adjuvant 
chemotherapy following surgery for localized disease.9,172 One such study 
of 11,871 patients with appendiceal neoplasms and cancers supported the 
use of adjuvant therapy for these patients, showing a benefit with adjuvant 
therapy in both mucinous and nonmucinous histologies.9 For stage I to III 
disease, in multivariate modeling, adjuvant chemotherapy improved OS for 
both mucinous (HR, 0.78; 95% CI, 0.68–0.89; P = .0002) and 
nonmucinous histologies (HR, 0.83; 95% CI, 0.74–0.94; P = 
.002). However, a more recent study of the NCBD showed differences in 
response to adjuvant therapy following RHC for non-metastatic mucinous 
and nonmucinous histologies.172 For nonmucinous histologies, adjuvant 
chemotherapy significantly improved OS for stage II and III disease, while 
for mucinous histologies, adjuvant therapy was associated with worse 
outcomes for stage I/II disease and no significant association with OS for 
stage III disease.
Several studies have looked at adjuvant treatment following RHC for GCA 
of the appendix, specifically.174-176 One such study, an 11-year analysis of 
867 patients with stage I–III GCA who underwent RHC identified within the 
NCDB, compared those who received adjuvant therapy to those who did 
not.175 Patients who received adjuvant therapy were more likely to be of 
younger age, male sex, and had a higher proportion of 
poorly/undifferentiated grade (27 vs. 5%; P < .001), T4 tumors (35 vs. 
11%; P < .001), and lymph node-positive disease (45 vs. 7%; P < .001) 
than patients who did not receive adjuvant therapy. After excluding 
patients without follow-up data, a survival advantage for adjuvant therapy

was detected only after stratification for lymph node-positive disease (P = 
.007). On Cox proportional hazard regression, adjuvant therapy 
demonstrated an independent association with improved OS (HR, 0.24; 
95% CI, 0.084–0.683; P = .007).
Other studies have investigated the role of adjuvant therapy following 
CRS/IPCT. A retrospective study of 103 patients with AA identified 68 who 
had a complete cytoreduction (CRS 0-1), of which 26 received adjuvant 
chemotherapy.169 The most commonly used regimens were capecitabine, 
CAPEOX (capecitabine/oxaliplatin), and FOLFOX 
(fluorouracil/LV/oxaliplatin). The median OS for patients with non-low-
grade and well-differentiated tumors who received adjuvant chemotherapy 
after complete cytoreduction was 9.03 years, compared to 2.88 years for 
patients who did not receive adjuvant chemotherapy (P = .02). For 
patients with low-grade disease, this benefit with adjuvant therapy was not 
observed. Another retrospective review matched 22 patients with low-
grade AC with MCP treated with CRS/HIPEC and perioperative systemic 
therapy with those who received CRS/HIPEC alone.177 Median OS was 
107 months for those who received perioperative systemic therapy 
compared to 72 months for those who did not (P = .46). Adjuvant systemic 
therapy was specifically associated with longer PFS (13.6 months) when 
compared to neoadjuvant therapy (6.8 months, P < .01). A third 
retrospective cohort study that compared survival of 77 patients with stage 
IV high-grade mucinous AC who were treated with CRS/HIPEC and 
adjuvant therapy to 103 patients who did not receive adjuvant therapy did 
not show a benefit.173 Median OS did not significantly differ with adjuvant 
chemotherapy (53 months with adjuvant chemotherapy vs. 75 months 
without), even after adjusting for cofounding variables. Adjuvant 
chemotherapy regimens used in this study included single-agent 
fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin-based, irinotecan-based, and 
bevacizumab-containing regimens.
Based on these mostly positive data, adjuvant systemic therapy with 
FOLFOX, CAPEOX, fluorouracil/LV, or capecitabine may be considered 
after RHC or CRS/IPCT for select patients. When adjuvant therapy is 
being considered, FOLFOX or CAPEOX are preferred over a single-agent 
fluoropyrimidine.
Palliative Systemic Therapy for Advanced or Metastatic 
Disease
Data supporting systemic therapy for advanced appendiceal neoplasms 
and cancers are limited and recommended therapies are, therefore, 
largely extrapolated from those studied in metastatic CRC. The goal of 
therapy is considered to be palliative in these settings. For some patients 
with advanced appendiceal neoplasms, particularly those with LAMN or 
HAMN, chemotherapy may cause significant side effects without clear 
benefit and should be used judiciously. Patients with positive lymph nodes 
may derive the most benefit from systemic therapy.178 The following 
sections discuss what data are available for systemic therapy in advanced 
appendiceal neoplasms and cancers and the Panel recommendations for 
treatment.

header: Systemic Therapy for LAMN or HAMN
content:
A randomized crossover trial included 24 patients with metastatic low-
grade mucinous appendiceal tumors who were not candidates for 
complete CRS and had radiographic imaging demonstrating the 
presence of mucinous PC.179 Patients on this trial were randomized to 
either 6 months of observation followed by 6 months of chemotherapy, or 
vice versa. Chemotherapy regimens that were used on the study varied, 
with most (63%) receiving single-agent fluorouracil or capecitabine; 13% 
receiving doublet chemotherapy [FOLFOX or FOLFIRI 
(fluorouracil/LV/irinotecan)]; and 21% receiving bevacizumab in 
combination with chemotherapy. Of the 15 patients who were available 
to be evaluated for the primary endpoint, tumor growth while receiving

chemotherapy increased 8.4% from baseline and was not significantly 
different from tumor growth during observation (4.0%; P = .26). Of the 18 
patients who received chemotherapy on the trial, none had an objective 
response and there was no significant difference in median OS between 
the observation-first group and the treatment-first group (76.0 vs. 53.2 
months; P = .48). Patient-reported quality of life metrics identified 
significantly worse fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and financial difficulty 
during treatment compared with observation.
A study of the NCDB reported similar findings in 639 patients with 
mucinous, grade 1, stage IV AC who were either treated with systemic 
chemotherapy or were not.180 Those who were treated with 
chemotherapy were found to have similar OS compared to those who did 
not receive chemotherapy after adjusting with a Cox proportional 
hazards model (HR, 1.1; 95% CI, 0.82–1.40). Another study of the NCDB 
reached the same conclusion for well-differentiated mucinous 
appendiceal neoplasms.9
While these studies demonstrated a lack of clinical benefit from systemic 
chemotherapy in patients with LAMN, some members of the Oncology Guidelines 
Panel have occasionally seen benefit from treating patients with systemic 
chemotherapy in this setting. Therefore, the Panel considers a trial of 
palliative systemic therapy to be a reasonable option for patients with 
metastatic LAMN or HAMN who are not candidates for surgery. Single-
agent fluoropyrimidine, FOLFOX, or FOLFIRI may be considered, with or 
without bevacizumab. Based on the previously discussed trial results, 
prolonged exposure to chemotherapy is not recommended for patients 
with LAMN or HAMN who are not demonstrating a clinical response.

header:
Systemic Therapy for Appendiceal Carcinomas (AA, GCA, or UC-
NOS)
content:
In contrast with the data for LAMN and HAMN, several studies have 
shown benefit for systemic therapy in patients with higher-risk AC.9,181-183
A single-center, observational study assessed the activity of systematic 
therapy with FOLFOX-4 in 20 patients with unresectable or relapsed 
PMP from appendiceal origin.184 The overall objective response rate 
(primary endpoint) was 20%. The median PFS and OS were 8 months 
(95% CI, 0–16.8 months) and 26.2 months (95% CI, 18.1–34.3 months), 
respectively. In this study, four individuals reported grade ≥3 AEs, 
including neutropenia (n = 3) and fatigue (n = 1). Other retrospective 
reviews have also identified FOLFOX and/or FOLFIRI as safe and 
effective options for treatment of unresectable AC.181,185-187
A retrospective review of 42 patients with high-grade AA with peritoneal 
metastases compared those receiving doublet chemotherapy (FOLFOX, 
CAPEOX, or FOLFIRI) to those receiving triplet chemotherapy 
[FOLFIRINOX or FOLFOXIRI (fluorouracil/LV/oxaliplatin/irinotecan)] as 
first-line therapy.188 Between the doublet and triplet groups, median OS 
was not significantly different (32.2 vs. 23.5 months; P = .38) and 
remained non-significantly different within a 1:1 propensity-matched cohort 
analysis. RFS after CRS/HIPEC and PFS were also similar between the 
groups.
A prospective, single-arm study of 15 patients with relapsed PMP of 
appendiceal origin treated with capecitabine and bevacizumab concluded 
that the combination was safe and effective for this disease.189 After a 
median follow-up of 12 months, median PFS was 8.2 months and 12-
month OS was 91%. All tumor samples except one had KRAS mutations, 
and GNAS mutations were correlated with a significantly shorter median 
PFS as compared to GNAS wild-type (P < .007). A retrospective review of 
130 patients with unresectable appendiceal tumors also showed an 
improvement in OS (P = .03) and PFS (P = .047) with the addition of 
bevacizumab to chemotherapy.190
Due to the limited data supporting systemic therapy for unresectable or 
metastatic appendiceal carcinomas, many of the recommendations are

extrapolated from CRC. First-line therapy may include a fluoropyrimidine-
based chemotherapy (CAPEOX, FOLFOX, FOLFIRI, or FOLFIRINOX) 
alone or in combination with bevacizumab. For patients for whom intensive 
therapy is not recommended, capecitabine or fluorouracil alone may be 
used, with or without bevacizumab. Based on data suggesting that anti- 
EGFR therapy does not improve OS for KRAS wild-type AA,191 cetuximab 
or panitumumab are not recommended as first-line options, except when 
given in combination with encorafenib for treatment of BRAF V600E 
tumors. As an extrapolation of the BREAKWATER trial for metastatic 
CRC,192 and data that BRAF V600E-mutant AA responds to BRAF-
targeted therapy,65 FOLFOX and encorafenib in combination with 
cetuximab or panitumumab may be given as first-line therapy for BRAF 
V600E AC. While dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutations with ultra-
hypermutated phenotype are rare in AC,191 checkpoint inhibitors are an 
option for any line of therapy in these patients.
For subsequent lines of therapy, the chemotherapy regimens 
recommended as first-line options may be given as subsequent line, if 
not given previously. In addition, as an extrapolation from CRC, many of 
the therapies recommended as options for non-first-line therapy in CRC 
may be considered as second- and subsequent-line options for AC. 
These options include targeted therapies for BRAF V600E, KRAS G12C, 
HER2 positivity, and NTRK or RET gene fusions. While there are data to 
suggest that the use of cetuximab or panitumumab have no impact on 
OS in RAS wild-type tumors,191 the Panel does not consider the evidence 
strong enough to preclude use of these therapies; therefore, they may be 
considered in combination with chemotherapy for KRAS/NRAS/BRAF 
wild-type AC in later lines of therapy.
There are several other systemic therapy regimens that have also been 
studied for unresectable or metastatic ACs. A phase II study of 40 
patients with advanced unresectable PMP studied a combination of
systemic MMC and capecitabine.193 Of 39 assessable patients on this 
trial, 38% showed benefit from the combination, including two patients 
with disease that was converted to resectability with potentially curable 
CRS. Taxane-based chemotherapy has also been of interest for 
treatment of advanced AA based on molecular features that distinguish it 
from advanced CRC, notably the rarity of APC mutation.51 A single-
center retrospective study looked at the efficacy of taxane chemotherapy 
on a cohort of 13 patients with metastatic AA.194 Median OS was 8.8 
months and, of 10 evaluable patients, there were three with disease 
response, four with stable disease, and three with progressive disease. A 
recent phase II study evaluated the efficacy and safety of atezolizumab 
in combination with bevacizumab in advanced, unresectable AA.195 Of 
the 16 patients treated with this combination, 100% experienced disease 
control (1 partial response, 15 stable disease), with a median PFS of 
18.3 months. Finally, another recent study studied the efficacy of the 
CDK4/6 inhibitor, palbociclib, in 16 patients with GNAS-mutant cancers 
with peritoneal mucinous carcinomatosis, 13 of those with appendiceal 
origin.196 Fifty percent of evaluable patients had stable disease after 12 
months of treatment and median survival had not yet been reached at 
17.6 months. While the Oncology Guidelines Panel finds these data encouraging, they 
do not consider the current evidence for these regimens to be strong 
enough to recommend at this time.
Post-Treatment Surveillance
Due to the rarity of the disease and the lack of data on optimal 
surveillance schedules for appendiceal neoplasms and cancers, the 
Oncology Guidelines recommendations for post-treatment surveillance are extrapolated 
from those recommended for colon cancer, with some adjustments based 
on the unique biology and recurrence patterns of appendiceal neoplasms 
and cancers.

LAMN or HAMN without high-risk peritoneal deposit have a more indolent 
disease course and, therefore, do not require as intensive surveillance 
compared to higher-risk tumors. Based on its indolent nature, LAMN and 
HAMN recurrences may occur later than other appendiceal tumors, 
necessitating a longer duration of surveillance. A retrospective review of 
217 patients with localized LAMN reported a median time to recurrence of 
14.4 months,197 while another retrospective study of noninvasive mucinous 
appendiceal neoplasms, with and without peritoneal dissemination, 
reported LAMN that recurred at 57 and 68 months post-treatment.198 
Based on these later recurrences for LAMN and HAMN, a longer 
surveillance interval of 10 years is recommended for both resected 
localized disease and resected metastatic disease.
For resected localized LAMN or HAMN, Oncology Guidelines recommends a history and 
physical exam, including measurement of tumor markers such as CEA, 
CA 19-9, and CA-125, every 6 to 12 months for 2 years, then annually for 
up to a total of 10 years. CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis should be 
performed every 6 to 12 months for 2 years from the date of surgery, then 
every 12 to 24 months for up to a total of 10 years. Surveillance 
recommendations are similar for resected metastatic LAMN or HAMN, 
except that physical exam should be at a frequency of every 6 months for 
the first 2 years and imaging should include the chest, abdomen, and 
pelvis and occur every 6 months for the first 2 years post-surgery, then 
every 12 months up to a total of 10 years. FDG-PET/CT is not indicated 
for surveillance of LAMN or HAMN, and colonoscopy need only be 
performed to look for additional primary cancers if no preoperative 
colonoscopy was done.
LAMN may be especially unlikely to recur. A study of 114 patients who 
underwent surgical resection of localized LAMN determined that after a 
mean follow-up duration of 4.7 years, no patients had experienced tumor 
recurrence.199 Another study of 217 patients with localized LAMN reported
a recurrence rate of just 3.2%, with many of the recurrent cases showing 
higher-risk features such as perforation or extra-appendiceal acellular 
mucin.197 Based on this, it may be appropriate to forgo surveillance for 
patients with completely resected LAMN confined to the appendix without 
high-risk features.
AC histologies such as AA, GCA, or UC-NOS carry a higher risk of 
recurrence and, therefore, prompt a more intensive post-treatment 
surveillance schedule.200 However, since recurrences tend to occur earlier 
than for LAMN or HAMN, 5 years is adequate for the surveillance period. 
For resected localized disease, history and physical examination, including 
measurement of CEA, CA 19-9 and CA-125, should be performed every 3-
6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years. After the 
first 2 years, tumor markers may be measured every 6 to 12 months. CT 
of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis should be performed every 6 to 12 
months from the date of surgery for a total of 5 years. For resected 
metastatic disease, history and physical exam, including measurement of 
CEA, CA 19-9 and CA-125, should be performed every 3 to 6 months for 2 
years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years. CT of the chest, 
abdomen, and pelvis should be performed every 3 to 6 months for 2 years, 
then every 6 to 12 months for a total of 5 years. FDG-PET/CT is not 
indicated for surveillance of AA, GCA, or UC-NOS, and colonoscopy need 
only be performed to look for additional primary cancers if no preoperative 
colonoscopy was done.
Survivorship
Based on the rarity of appendiceal neoplasms and cancers, there is a 
dearth of data regarding survivorship for this disease. The Panel 
recommendations for survivorship are largely extrapolated from the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Colon Cancer, although some special considerations may 
be needed for survivors experiencing long-term effects of CRS and/or 
IPCT for peritoneal metastases.201,202 This section will provide an overview

of the Panel recommendations for survivorship; for more detailed 
information, see the Survivorship section in the Oncology Guidelines for 
Colon Cancer (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
The Panel recommends that a prescription for survivorship and transfer 
of care to the primary care physician be written.203 The oncologist and 
primary care provider should have defined roles in the surveillance 
period, with roles communicated to the patient. Other recommendations 
for survivors of GI cancers include monitoring for late or long-term 
sequelae of treatment, such as oxaliplatin-induced peripheral 
neuropathy, fatigue, pain, sexual dysfunction, and emotional or social 
distress.204-208 Specific management interventions to address these and 
other side effects are described in a review.209 Disease preventive 
measures, such as immunizations; early disease detection through 
periodic screening for second primary cancers (eg, breast, cervical, or 
prostate cancers); and routine good medical care and monitoring are 
recommended. The Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines) provide screening, evaluation, and treatment 
recommendations for common consequences of cancer and cancer 
treatment to aid health care professionals who work with survivors of 
adult-onset cancer in the post-treatment period, including those in 
specialty cancer survivor clinics and primary care practices.
Summary
Appendiceal neoplasms and cancers are rare tumors of the digestive 
system, often found incidentally on surgical specimens following 
appendectomy. Treatment recommendations depend on the pathologic 
type, with some tumors (ie, LAMN or HAMN) presenting a lower risk of 
progression or recurrence than other pathologic types. For localized 
disease, surgery via a negative-margin appendectomy with or without 
cecal cuff resection is often curative, although adjuvant chemotherapy 
may be considered for higher-risk pathologies. Peritoneal disease will
often require CRS, with or without IPCT, or palliative measures if 
complete CRS is not possible. Cases with a low-grade primary tumor 
histology but higher-grade peritoneal metastasis should be treated like 
other high-risk pathologies. The role of neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy 
is poorly defined but may be recommended depending on the specific 
clinical scenario. Treatment for extraperitoneal metastatic disease is 
palliative and may include a combination of surgery, systemic therapy, 
clinical trial, or best supportive care.
